definition: *Vitriol* is the alchemists' name for the sulphate salts — most familiarly blue vitriol (copper sulphate) and green vitriol (iron sulphate) — glassy, brightly colored crystals, and the corrosive acids distilled from them. It was a workhorse reagent: ground for pigments, roasted and distilled to yield mineral acids. Where an amalgam is soft and metallic, vitriol is a crystalline salt, prized for its color and its bite.
history: Medieval Latin *vitriolum*, from Latin *vitrum*, 'glass,' named the salt for its clear, glassy crystals. The turn runs two ways. In the laboratory vitriol became a master material — the *Picatrix* grinds blue vitriol and talc for a golden pigment, and alchemists coded it as the 'Green Lion,' a key material in the Work. In speech the same corrosive salt gave us *vitriolic*: sharp, acid words, as when a humanist's vitriolic *elogium* or a scholar's vitriolic pen burned an opponent. The glassy salt still bites in the metaphor.

def_sources.label: Attrell & Porreca, Picatrix: A Medieval Treatise on Astral Magic
Zambelli, White Magic, Black Magic in the European Renaissance

etymology.origin: Medieval Latin vitriolum 'sulphate', from Latin vitrum 'glass' (for its glassy crystals)
etymology.note: The alchemists' motto V.I.T.R.I.O.L. spelled a whole instruction; 'vitriolic' speech still carries the acid.
